items.myth: Cross-entropy and KL divergence are the same thing.
items.reality: D_KL(P || Q) = H(P, Q) − H(P). They differ by the entropy of P. When P is fixed (e.g. dataset labels), minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to minimizing KL, but the numeric values differ.
items.example: Fair coin P vs Q with q(H)=0.9: H(P,Q) ≈ 1.74 bits, H(P)=1 bit, D_KL(P||Q) ≈ 0.74 bits.

items.myth: Lower cross-entropy always means higher accuracy.
items.reality: A model can be accurate yet poorly calibrated (high loss on low-confidence correct calls), or vice versa. Cross-entropy measures probabilistic quality on true classes, not just argmax correctness.
items.example: Two models both predict the right class; one outputs 0.51 vs 0.99 on the true label — same accuracy, different cross-entropy.

items.myth: Cross-entropy only applies to one-hot hard labels.
items.reality: Any valid PMF P works: label smoothing, soft targets, and teacher distributions in distillation are cross-entropy with general p(x).
items.example: P = [0.05, 0.05, 0.9] with softmax Q gives −Σ p_k log q_k, not just −log q_3.
